coredata.dc:title: Solution-based carbon quantum dots as electrochemical “on–off” switch in aqueous medium: Engineering of a sustainable green sensor and its computational investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Chacko, Anu Rose
Narayanan, Radhika
Korah, Binila K.
Mathew, Sneha
Abraham, Thomas
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: It is crucial to establish sensitive electroanalysis of pollutants in water environments, but this is difficult due to their redox inertness and uncertain detection methods. We herein report an electrochemical switch ‘on–off sensor for the selective detection of Hg(II) and amoxicillin (AMX) based on a switch platform system. Our design used a carbon quantum dot (CQD)-based sensing probe (SP) to intensify the electrochemical signal due to its excellent conductivity. The “switch-on” state was attained by introducing the Hg(II) ions and sensing probe (SP) onto the electrode surface to facilitate electron transfer. The modified electrode signifies an excellent electrocatalytic activity of Hg (II), giving sharp anodic and cathodic peaks at 0.53 and 0.42 (V vs. SCE), respectively. Upon addition of AMX, it cleaves the SP into fragments from the sensing interface, hindering the electrochemical signal to create a “switch-off” state. As expected, the SP displayed a desirable linear response range from 0.0 to 100 nM and the detection limit down to 15.0 nM. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations confirm that the binding energy of the AMX with the Hg(II) ion is greater than that of SP and Hg(II). Moreover, the probe showed excellent stability and reproducibility with Hg(II) selectively; thus, it confines great scope for application in environmental water sciences. Further, the switch “on–off” sensing behavior of SP providing two chemical inputs (Hg (II) and AMX) helps to set up an INHIBIT logic gate. A series of experiments, including DFT and electrochemistry, demonstrate the detection mechanism and enhanced electrochemical sensitivity. This sustainable engineering approach creates a perfect sensor using a carbon quantum dots-based sensing probe from waste paper.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Electrochemical
Sensing probe
Switch
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and optical characteristics of CQD-based SP
Electrochemical response of SP towards metal ions
Electrochemical response of SP towards drugs
Binding energies for the complexes of glucose with Hg(II)
Binding energies for the complexes of AMX with (i) Hg(II), (ii) glucose and, (iii) glucose@Hg(II)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) HR-TEM images of the SP with different magnifications, (c) diameter distribution, (d) UV–vis. spectrum, (e) FT-IR spectrum, and (f) XRD pattern of SP.
(a) Raman spectra, (b) XPS survey spectra, (c) C 1s peaks, and (d) O 1s peaks of SP.

(a) Conductivity investigation of (i) GCE; (ii) Hg(II)/GCE; (iii) SP@GCE; (iv) Hg(II)/SP@GCE; in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 6.0); scan rate of 50 mV/s, and (b) DPV response of various concentrations of Hg(II) (0–100 nM) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.0). inset; CVs obtained SP modified electrode containing different concentrations of Hg(II) (0–100 µM) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.0) at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 and calibration plot of I/mA vs. conc. [Hg(II)]/nM. All the DPV responses were detected in 0.1 M PBS solution.

(a) DPV response of SP at various concentrations of AMX (0–20 nM) and 100 nM Hg(II) in 0.1 M PBS pH 6.0. inset; CVs of SP at various concentrations of AMX (0–20 nM) and 100 nM Hg(II) in 0.1 M PBS pH 6.0; scan rate of 50 mVs−1, (b) Calibration plot of I vs. conc. [AMX], (c) The influence of various coexisting drugs on anti-interference of AMX by Hg(II)/SP complex in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.0) (Blk is 20 nM Hg(II) and 20 nM AMX, the concentration of other anions are 20 nM, I0 and I are the peak current of sample SP in the absence and the presence of AMX), and (d) EIS plots of SP/GCE, Hg(II)@SP/GCE, AMX@Hg(II)/SP/GCE in 0.1 M PBS solution.

Optimized structures of (a) glucose with charge distribution, (b) glucose@Hg(II) complex, (c) AMX with charge distribution; (d) AMX@Hg(II) complex, and (e) AMX@glucose complex (bond lengths are in Å).
Diagram for the preparation of Hg(II) and AMX sensors.
Analysis of water and fish samples.
Calculated binding energies (Ebind) and the selected structural parameters of the complexes of glucose and amoxicillin with Hg (II) in the water solvent system.

head.ce:title: Solution-based carbon quantum dots as electrochemical “on–off” switch in aqueous medium: Engineering of a sustainable green sensor and its computational investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills PO, Kottayam, Kerala 686560, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills PO, Kottayam, Kerala 686560, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is crucial to establish sensitive electroanalysis of pollutants in water environments, but this is difficult due to their redox inertness and uncertain detection methods. We herein report an electrochemical switch ‘on–off sensor for the selective detection of Hg(II) and amoxicillin (AMX) based on a switch platform system. Our design used a carbon quantum dot (CQD)-based sensing probe (SP) to intensify the electrochemical signal due to its excellent conductivity. The “switch-on” state was attained by introducing the Hg(II) ions and sensing probe (SP) onto the electrode surface to facilitate electron transfer. The modified electrode signifies an excellent electrocatalytic activity of Hg (II), giving sharp anodic and cathodic peaks at 0.53 and 0.42 (V vs. SCE), respectively. Upon addition of AMX, it cleaves the SP into fragments from the sensing interface, hindering the electrochemical signal to create a “switch-off” state. As expected, the SP displayed a desirable linear response range from 0.0 to 100 nM and the detection limit down to 15.0 nM. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations confirm that the binding energy of the AMX with the Hg(II) ion is greater than that of SP and Hg(II). Moreover, the probe showed excellent stability and reproducibility with Hg(II) selectively; thus, it confines great scope for application in environmental water sciences. Further, the switch “on–off” sensing behavior of SP providing two chemical inputs (Hg (II) and AMX) helps to set up an INHIBIT logic gate. A series of experiments, including DFT and electrochemistry, demonstrate the detection mechanism and enhanced electrochemical sensitivity. This sustainable engineering approach creates a perfect sensor using a carbon quantum dots-based sensing probe from waste paper.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The elemental composition of the earth and dosage of antibiotics for global needs should be maintained within a reasonable range. Combined heavy metals and antibiotic contamination in aqueous environments severely harm ecosystems and common health [1,2]. The irresponsible disposal of domestic and hospital waste, commercial and agricultural runoff, mass manufacturing, and excessive utilization of human medicine, livestock, and aquaculture cause pollution in the surface or groundwater. Wherein, heavy metals of Hg(II), as well as antibiotics of amoxicillin (AMX), are the most typical sorts [3,4]. The presence of mercury ions and amoxicillin in controlled quantities will be essential in various fields. The effects of mercury liability can be very severe and subtle. Subjection to methyl mercury frequently occurs when people eat types of fish and shellfish. People exposed to the accumulation of mercury at high levels through the food chain may experience adverse health effects of permanent brain damage and other chronic diseases [5,6]. Amoxicillin is a penicillin antibiotic, that is used to treat bacterial infections of the ear, nose, skin, throat, and urinary tract as well as bronchitis and pneumonia. However, excessive amoxicillin can cause potential side effects such as dermatitis, acute interstitial nephritis, and panic disorder [7]. Therefore, a massive demand for developing methodologies to remove and sensitive detection of Hg(II) and AMX in aqueous media is a great treat in environmental water and food monitoring, as well as clinical toxicology [8].

Several sensing probes are known for quantitative detection of Hg(II) and AMX through various strategies such as electrochemical [9,10] and optical sensing [11] and imaging-assisted sensing [12]. Electrochemical sensing has attracted significant attention among these methods due to its good sensitivity, fast response, simplified operation, and cost-effectiveness. Until today, no other probes have been explored to unitedly monitor Hg(II) and AMX in electrochemical sensing via a switch 'on–off' detection platform. Therefore, inspired by the challenges and research interest in designing such smart probes, we focused on developing a green, metal-free, economical, and practical one-step hydrothermal synthesized sensing probe.

Recently, zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials have performed well in electrochemical sensing due to their high solubility, nontoxicity, ease of modification, and strong electrochemical activity [13–15]. From this outlook, we describe a carbon quantum dot-based sensing probe (SP) prepared by a hydrothermal method using waste paper as the carbon source. Due to economic and environmental benefits, several research groups have worked to convert lower-quality waste paper typically containing 90–99% cellulose into value-added products [16–18]. We proposed a method to solve the disposal of environmental pollution by recycling waste paper into electrochemical “on–off” sensors. In place of hazardous chemical compounds, expensive laboratory equipment, and complex reaction procedures, the synthesis of low-cost and non-hazardous carbon quantum dots-based sensing probe (SP) is commendable. It detects heavy metal ions like Hg(II) in the water samples with lower detection limits. The detection limit attained was 15.0 nM, which is below the recommended standard set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) for drinkable water (2 µg/L) [19]. As depicted in Scheme 1 , fabricated SP was deposited on the glassy carbon electrode surface (SP@GCE), where the anodic peak current signal of SP was enhanced by the electron conductivity of Hg(II) ions (the “on” state). When the AMX was added to the modified electrode surface with Hg(II) ions, it spontaneously interacted with Hg(II) and so AMX/Hg(II) was detached from the SP@GCE and retrieved the initial signal (the “off” state). To supplement the experimental findings for the selective 'on–off' sensing of Hg(II) and AMX, we investigated the binding energy between a single unit of the as-prepared SP with Hg(II) and AMX by a density functional theory (DFT) study. Consequently, combining the excellent performance of fabricated SP opened new perspectives for developing highly selective sensing probes.

SP was synthesized by a facile hydrothermal treatment of waste newspaper. Briefly, 3.0 g newspaper was mixed with 100 mL of double distilled water. After 30 min sonication and stirring, it was placed into a 100 mL Teflon lining stainless-steel autoclave and thermalized for 10 h at 180 °C. The oven was cooled to room temperature. The resultant brownish solution was transferred to a strainer tube and centrifuged at 32000 rpm for 20 min. The aqueous solution was collected by filtration with a 0.22 µm membrane filter to remove unreacted agents (Scheme S1).
Instrumentation, electrochemical treatment for detection, and real sample analysis experiments are shown in the Supplementary information.

SP was experimentally synthesized from wastepaper which contains 90–99% cellulose. Glucose (β-form) is chosen as the single unit compound in the computational study as it is the cellulose monomer and an important intermediate formed during the hydrolysis of cellulose [20]. The quantum chemical calculations were performed on the glucose and their analyte (Hg(II) and AMX) complexes, using the density functional theory (DFT) method as employed in Gaussian 16 code, and the results were visualized using Gauss View 6 [21,22]. The initial structures of the glucose and AMX were obtained from the X-ray crystallographic data [23,24]. The systematic rotor search implemented in the Avogadro software was employed to find the lowest energy structures of glucose, AMX, and their complexes [25]. These molecular geometries were optimized using the B3LYP functional in the Gaussian 16 software [26–28]. The LANL2DZ basis set was used for Hg, while the 6-31+G(d) basis set was used for all other atoms [29]. The geometry optimizations were performed in the water solvent system using the continuum SMD solvent model [30]. The vibrational frequencies were computed at the same level of the theory to confirm that the molecules have adopted stable geometries. The electronic charge distribution of the glucose and AMX were analyzed by natural population analysis (NPA) in Gaussian 16 software [31].

The binding energy between the glucose and Hg(II), AMX and Hg(II), glucose and AMX, glucose-Hg(II) complex, and AMX were calculated using the Eqs. (1)–(4), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characteristics of CQD-based SP
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological and microstructural composition of prepared SP were characterized by various techniques to highlight their properties. First, the morphology and size of the SP were investigated by HR-TEM. Ultrasonication of SP solution followed by centrifugation and filtration produces homogenous and well-dispersed nature in water. The bright-field images of amorphous carbon-based samples are rarely visible due to the low variance between SP and SP-coated copper grids (Fig. 1 (a), (b)). The apparently amorphous SP was spherical in shape with a narrow size distribution ranging from 3.0 to 7.0 nm and with an average size of 4.87 nm (Fig. 1(c)). To examine the optical properties of SP, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis.) absorption spectra were measured accordingly. As shown in Fig. 1(d), the optical absorption peak of the SP was observed at 205 nm and 275 nm corresponding to the π → π* transition of C=C and n → π* transition of the C=O band [32]. FT-IR further identified the functional groups and chemical compositions of SP. Fig. 1(e) shows the presence of oxygen functionalities of different types in SP was confirmed by peaks at 3228 cm−1 (O–H stretching vibrations), 2940 cm−1 (C–H stretching vibrations), 2197 cm−1 (carboxylic group, COO–) [33], 1560 cm−1 (C=C stretching vibrations) [34], 1380 cm−1 (C–H bending vibrations) [35], and 1056 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibrations). The strong amorphous character of the SP was also reflected in the broad peak between 20° and 40° 2θ, which is referred to the (002) plane of graphene in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) [36] profile shown in Fig. 1(f). Such a peak has been far observed in XRD patterns of amorphous carbon.

Raman spectrum of SP (Fig. 2 (a)) shows a D band at 1388 cm−1, which is attributed to amorphous carbon due to the peak’s width, and a G band at 1584 cm−1, which belongs to the first-order scattering vibration mode E2g in the graphite sheet [37]. The spectrum was fitted using a Gaussian function. The XPS measurements were carried out to elucidate the chemical state of elements and relative composition by atomic layers present in SP. Fig. 2(b) displays two dominant peaks at 284.2 and 532.3 eV, which are attributed to C1s and O1s, suggesting the existence of C and O elements. The fraction of C and O elements are calculated to be 63.2% and 36.8%, respectively. The C/O ratio in SP is observed to be 1.71, indicating a deep involvement of oxygen-containing functional groups such as carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the exterior of SP [38]. The deconvolution of the C and O peaks provides further information on the potential chemical species in each main element. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(c)) can be resolved into three different carbon states at 284.2, 285.9, and 288.8 eV, which correspond to C=C, C–O–C, and C=O, respectively. The deconvolution of O 1s shows peaks at 531.6 and 532.2 eV, which are associated with C=O and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively (Fig. 2(d)). To further explore the surface information change of SP after adding Hg(II) ions were performed in Fig. S1. It is clearly that there is a changes in the binding energies of the carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen atoms in SP. The binding energy of C=C, C–O–C, and C=O of C 1s are 282.5, 283.9, and 286.1 eV, respectively, and the atoms involved are shifted to lower energy levels (Fig. S1(b)). This lower binding energy enhances the transfer of an electron from that atom to another atom. Because its electrons are loosely held by the nucleus, and it would require less energy to move an electron. The similar lower energy shift of C=O and C–OH/C–O–C of O 1 s prove the same result as the above (Fig. S1(c)). Hg 4f spectra are illustrated in Fig. S1(d). Two main peaks located at 99.4 and 103 eV can correspond to Hg 4f7/2 and Hg 4f5/2 components.

It is widely established that parameters like the nature of supporting electrolytes, pH level, and deposition volume will significantly impact the experimental circumstances and the detection signal. The best experimental conditions for switch 'on–off' detection of Hg(II) and AMX under different modified GCEs were obtained through a series of practical optimization. The anodic peak currents of Hg(II) measured in two different supporting electrolytes (0.1 M)-phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and acetate buffer solutions (ABS)-are compared in Fig. S2(a). 0.1 M PBS was selected for further investigation due to its high current response. The pH selectivity of the probe in the different 0.1 M PBS with pH ranges from 4.0 to 9.0. As shown in Fig. S2(b), the peak current signal (Ipa ) increased with the pH over the range from 4.0 to 6.0 and then decreased dramatically. Such a result might be since a slightly acidic environment could stabilize the SP, and higher pH would allow the metal hydroxide formation, which results in precipitation and affects the accuracy of the sensor [39]. Regarding the sensitivity of the SP, we selected 6.0 for further experiments. SP depends on the pH environment, so the sensing activity may also depend on the pH. Also, Fig. S2(c) analyzed the effect of modifier volume (5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, and 25.0 µL) on the electrochemical response to Hg(II). Peak current increases rapidly between 5.0 and 10.0 µL and gradually reduces between 10.0 and 25.0 µL. The adsorption capacity of Hg(II) can be greatly increased with an increase in the sample volume. With the increase in sample volume, the electrochemical adsorption and redox on the electrode surface reached the maximum equilibrium. The decrease in stripping current was due to the Hg(II) hindrance on the electrode surface. Therefore, we optimized experimental conditions in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 6.0), with a deposition volume of 10.0 µL SP on GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical response of SP towards metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: First, we screened the probe's affinity toward different metal ions in PBS solution. The cyclic voltammetry signal of SP for Hg (II) was relatively high over other competitive metals with the same concentration (Fig. S3(a), (b)). The change in the electrochemical property of the bare glassy carbon electrode with SP towards the presence and absence of 100 nM Hg(II) have been then studied. Fig. 3 (a) explains the peak-to-peak separation changes and oxidation peak current along with the addition of Hg(II). It can be seen that there was no existing pair of redox peaks of bare GCE due to its electrical resistance. After SP was decorated on the electrode surface, a slightly increased redox peak current was obtained during the scan. The “switch-on” state was explained from the CV profile of both electrodes. It showed the Hg(II) redox signals recorded at SP@GCE were much higher than that of the bare GCE electrode. The modification of GCE with SP enhances the electrochemical property of the electrode, but bare GCE diminishes it and becomes non-conductive.

To check the effect of various concentrations of Hg(II) on the current signal, the proposed sensor probe was incubated with 0.1 M PBS containing a different concentration of Hg(II). The DPV response was employed to investigate the sensitivity of the SP. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the oxidation peak current was improved evenly by increasing the Hg(II) concentrations, ranging from 0.0 to 100 nM. Based on the calibration plot of Fig. 3(b), the linear regression equation was I = 0.06103 [Hg(II)/nM] + 5.3830 with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.982. The value of the limit of detection and limit of quantification was 15.0 nM and 45.5 nM, referenced by the 3σ/m and 10σ/m rule, respectively [40,41]. Here σ denotes the standard deviation obtained from regression analysis, and m is the slope of the linear fit. Again, the sensitivity of the SP is solely determined by the slope of the calibration curve, which is 0.0610 µA per nM in this case. It indicates a good detection sensitivity of the proposed sensor.

CV curves of SP with 100 nM Hg(II) in 0.1 M PBS at different scan rates (10–100 mVs−1) were shown in Fig. S4. The redox peak potential moves in positive and negative directions with the increment in scanning rate. There is a linear relationship between the redox peak current and the scanning rate, indicating the effect of concentration and diffusional properties of the Hg(II) ions as suggested by the Randles–Ševčík equation. Fig. S5 shows the photoelectrochemical behavior of SP with successive additions of Hg(II) illuminated by light switched on and off. The current increases linearly by adding Hg(II) under each irradiation and decreases rapidly after the light is off. It means that the photocurrent varies in direct proportion to the output power of the light source. The Hg(II) concentration and response current calibration curve show an excellent linear relationship at the Hg(II) concentration from 0.0 to 100 nM. In addition, to compare the analytical parameters of the sensor probe, the previous reports about the detection of Hg(II) are listed with references in Table S1. This demonstrates that the present method highlights the efficiency of the proposed SP@GCE electrode with excellent selectivity for determining Hg(II).

Stability and reproducibility were crucial considerations when assessing the practical side of the newly developed electrode [42]. The long-term stability of SP@GCE was investigated and stored in 0.1 M PBS at room temperature. The anodic peak signal of Hg(II) was monitored daily using the same SP@GCE. After 25 days, the CV response of the same SP@GCE has maintained about 91.78% from the initial current response (Fig. S6). These results proved that SP@GCE has good storage stability. Investigating the electrode's reproducibility is essential. Four distinct SP-modified GCEs were used to explore reproducibility after 5 days of standing, and the results show that they exhibit comparable response current levels to 100 nM Hg(II) with a low relative standard deviation (RSD, 1.23 %) (Fig. S7). Fig. S6(b) shows the anti-interferential experiments performed with interferents. The results revealed that the redox signal of SP@GCE in the presence of Hg(II) was unchanged in other coexisting metals, suggesting the excellent anti-interference capacity for practical detection. Based on the excellent sensitivity and high selectivity of SP towards Hg(II), we investigated the recovery tests in water and fish samples. The mean results are tabulated in Table 1 . These acceptable results suggested the proposed SP possessed promising potential in real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical response of SP towards drugs
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the affinity of SP towards different types of drugs such as ciprofloxacin (CIP), levofloxacin (LEVO), vancomycin (VCM), amoxicillin (AMX), cloxacillin (CLOX), cefalexin (CFX), kanamycin (KAN), doxycycline (DOC), tetracycline (TC), and paclitaxel (PTX) the CV and DPV estimations were received under similar experimental conditions. Notably, the SP showed insignificant behavior for the tested drugs (Fig. S8). However, we found that the anodic peak current of Hg(II)/SP in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.0) selectively decreases in the presence of AMX out of other drugs (Fig. S9(a), (b)). This may be connected to Hg(II) displacement from its probable Hg(II)/SP complex, which suggests the SP could function as a ‘switch-off’ electrochemical sensor selectively for the detection of AMX. Considering this stance, some quantitative analysis of the Hg(II)/SP complex towards AMX was explored by the DPV analysis. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the DPV response of the original Hg(II)/SP complex in 0.1 M PBS is high, while after the introduction of AMX, the peak current decreased linearly. It exhibits an excellent linear correlation in the range of 0–20 nM between the pulse current signal and the AMX concentrations (Fig. 4(b)). The detection limit of the Hg(II)/SP complex system for sensing AMX reached 3.02 nM.

The different drugs were employed in the test to interpret the anti-interference character of the Hg(II)/SP complex toward AMX. Even in the presence of these co-occurring drugs, Hg(II)/SP complex detects AMX with absolute precision. (Fig. 4(c)). This implies that the Hg(II)/SP complex can be used as a highly selective sensor for AMX with an intriguing anti-interference character. With the addition of Hg(II) to the AMX/Hg(II)/SP complex, the peak current regained and was almost similar to the intensity of a complex Hg(II)/SP and reached a ‘switch-on’ state. With the alternative introduction of Hg(II)(20 nM) and AMX(20 nM) for about eight cycles, the typical SP exhibits the reversibly switchable sensing nature (switch-on–off) without any considerable loss in peak current (Fig. S10). Moreover, Fig. 4(d) shows the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots of the proposed SP. An ample semicircle of SP was decorated on the GCE electrode surface owing to the high electron-transfer resistance (Ret ). After the introduction of Hg(II) on the SP@GCE surface, the value of Ret decreased because of the conductive and electron transfer nature of Hg(II). The Ret of AMX/Hg(II)/SP slightly increased, which was attributed to the electron transfer hindrance of AMX. Combining the CV and EIS characterization results, the developed SP could function as an electrochemical 'on–off' switch.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding energies for the complexes of glucose with Hg(II)
ce:section.ce:para: The natural population analysis (NPA) charges on the β-D-glucose shows that the oxygen atoms of the hydroxyl group possess higher electronic charge (−0.8008 to −0.8268) than the ether oxygen atom (−0.6381) (Fig. 5 (a)). The Hg(II) ion can bind with these electron-rich oxygen atoms through four possible sites such as (1) O1, O2 (2) O3, O4 (3) O4, O6, and (4) O5, O6. The optimized geometries of β-D-glucose and the corresponding glucose@Hg(II) complexes are shown in the Fig. 5(a) and (b) and S11. The calculated binding energies of the glucose@Hg(II) complexes and the bond distance between the binding atoms in the most stable complex are summarized in the Table 2 and S2. The highest binding energy is possessed by the complex in which the Hg(II) ion interact with O3 and O4 atoms of β-D-glucose (Fig. 5(b)). Hence, Hg(II) ion prefer to bind with O3 and O4 atoms of β-D-glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding energies for the complexes of AMX with (i) Hg(II), (ii) glucose and, (iii) glucose@Hg(II)
ce:section.ce:para: The most stable structures of AMX, AMX@Hg(II), and AMX@glucose complexes are shown in Fig. 5. The NPA charges on the AMX (Fig. 5(c)), indicates that there are five sites through which AMX can potentially bind with Hg(II) ion. The possible binding sites on the AMX are (1) O4 and N10 (2) O2 (3) O3 and O5 (4) O6 (5) N5 and O7. DFT calculations shown that the AMX@Hg(II) complex was stable only when Hg(II) ion bind with O4 and N10 atoms of AMX (Fig. 5(d)). Table 2 shows that the binding energy of the AMX@Hg(II) complex is −14.25 kJmol−1. The AMX can bind with glucose and the most stable structure has a hydrogen bond between carbonyl oxygen atom (O4) of amoxicillin and hydrogen atom (H51) of hydroxyl group in glucose (Fig. 5(e)). Fig. S11 display other possible orientations of AMX@glucose and their corresponding binding energies are summarized in Table S3. Table 2 indicates that the binding energy of the AMX@Hg(II) complex is greater than that of AMX@glucose. The interaction between AMX and glucose@Hg(II) complex was analyzed using the DFT calculation. However, in the case of AMX@Hg(II)-glucose complex, positive binding energy was obtained (Table S4 and Fig. S11).

The order of stability of the complexes based on the binding energies is AMX@Hg(II)-glucose < glucose@Hg(II) < AMX@glucose < AMX@Hg(II). The calculated binding energies suggest that the proposed electrochemical switch ‘on-off’ mechanism was successful.

Carbon quantum dot-based sensing probe (SP) have a high surface area and a large number of functional groups on their surface, which can bind to Hg(II) ions through various interactions such as electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and coordination. So, this is a promising area of research with potential applications in environmental monitoring and remediation. After adding AMX, Hg(II) ions can form covalent bonds with various chemical groups, including amino and carboxyl groups found in AMX. One possible interaction between AMX and Hg(II) ions is the formation of a complex between the two molecules. This complex could potentially alter the pharmacological properties of AMX, leading to changes in its efficacy or toxicity. Additionally, Hg(II) ions could potentially react with the amine or carboxyl groups on AMX, leading to the formation of new chemical species with altered properties.

Various sustainable molecular switches can be obtained from the distinctive modification in the electrochemical signals of the sensing probes upon interaction with distinct guest species. An INHIBIT gate was conducted in the sensing probe by employing Hg(II) and AMX inputs. As shown in Fig. S12, a more considerable switch-on signal was obtained with Hg(II) (input: 1/0) than with other inputs (0/0, 0/1, 1/1), and the only addition of AMX and its interaction with Hg(II)/SP caused the switch-off state. These results were under the correct execution of the Boolean INHIBIT logic gate, which is represented by where the output is “1” only if one specific input is “1” and the others are “0”. According to the truth table (Fig. S12(b)), successive logic operations Hg(II)/SP displayed high current, although the low current was obtained when the applied logic inputs were SP, AMX/SP, AMX/Hg(II)/SP, respectively. In this study, we constructed an INHIBIT logic gate (Fig. S12(a)) corresponding to the truth table using elementary logic gates such as NOT and an AND gate.

In conclusion, an electrochemical sensing probe composed of the carbon quantum dot is developed to detect trace Hg(II) and AMX in water samples through an 'on–off' switch platform. The excellent electrochemical performance is examined from the standpoint of electron transfer. The first “switch on” state was accomplished based on the introduction of Hg(II) on the SP@GCE surface. The SP exhibits an excellent ability to detect Hg(II) with a broad dynamic range of 0.0–100 nM and LOD of 15.0 nM. Then, the “switch-off” state was obtained after adding AMX, the peak current can be distinctly lowered due to the formation and free of the AMX/Hg(II) complex from the surface of SP@GCE. The designed sensing probe can effectively detect AMX in the concentration range of 0.0–20 nM with a LOD of 3.02 nM. In addition, the prepared SP showed reversible switching properties for sensing Hg(II) and AMX. They have high reproducibility and long-term stability (>25 days), and they can also be used to record Hg(II) ions in real aqueous samples. The DFT calculated binding energies of the complexes confirm the proposed electrochemical switch ‘on-off’ mechanism was successful with the order AMX@Hg(II)-glucose < glucose@Hg(II) < AMX@glucose < AMX@Hg(II). The electrochemical “on–off” switching behavior of SP in the presence of two chemical inputs, Hg(II) and AMX, has been utilized to design an INHIBIT logic gate. This proof-of-concept study demonstrated the CQD-based sensing probe for detecting Hg(II) and AMX. This study may offer a new way to develop cost-effective and environmentally friendly sensing probes for detecting and diagnosing metal ions and antibiotics in green chemistry and environmental applications.

Anu Rose Chacko: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Radhika Narayanan: DFT study. Binila K. Korah: Data curation. Sneha Mathew: Data curation. Thomas Abraham: Data curation. Beena Mathew: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108853.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
